english: Hit the Autobahn with the whole crew. Learn travel and geography vocabulary. Discover Germany beyond Berlin.
Learn 18 words about travel and geography
Experience the Autobahn with Lukas, Mia, Hildi, and Verumius
Discover Germany's diversity

Saturday morning. A rented car is parked outside Verumius's building. Lukas is behind the wheel, sunglasses on, playlist ready. Mia opens a paper map because she insists. Hildi climbs into the back, already suspicious. Verumius throws his bag in the trunk and grins.
So, folks. Freiburg. Six hours. Autobahn. No speed limit. Are you ready?
There IS a speed limit, Lukas. You just drive too fast.
Hildi, on the A5 there are sections without a speed limit. Lukas is right. But we will make stops along the way.
I have never been on the Autobahn! And never in southern Germany. Are we going to travel through the whole state?
federal state
Bund (federation) + Land (state/country). Germany has 16\nBundesländer, each with its own government and school system.

A: In which federal state is Freiburg?
B: In Baden-Württemberg.
A: How many federal states does Germany have?
B: Sixteen, from Bavaria to Bremen.

Bund (federation) + Land (state/country). Germany has 16 Bundesländer, each with its own government, police, and school system. Berlin is both a city AND a Bundesland. Bavaria is the largest, Bremen the smallest.
region
From Latin 'regio' (direction/district). Germany's regions\ndiffer wildly: coastal Friesland, alpine Bavaria, industrial Ruhr.

A: Which region of Germany do you like best?
B: The southwest. The landscape is beautiful.
A: Every region has its own dialect.
B: And its own food!

From Latin 'regio' (direction/district). Same word in English. Germany's regions differ wildly: coastal Friesland, alpine Bavaria, industrial Ruhrgebiet, wine-country Rhineland. Each has its own dialect and food.
transit, passage
Durch (through) + Fahrt (drive/trip). 'Keine Durchfahrt'\nis one of Germany's most common road signs.

A: Is transit allowed here?
B: No, no through traffic.
A: Then we have to go around.
B: Yes, take the next exit.
Durch (through) + Fahrt (drive/trip). A transit, a passage. 'Keine Durchfahrt' (No through traffic) is one of Germany's most common road signs. The word tells you: you drive THROUGH, not stop.
motorway service station
Rast (rest) + Stätte (place). German Raststätten are an\ninstitution: overpriced coffee, Currywurst, and paid toilets.

A: I need a break. Is there a service station?
B: Yes, in ten kilometers.
A: Do service stations have good coffee?
B: No, but clean toilets.
Rast (rest) + Stätte (place). German Raststätten are an institution: overpriced coffee, Currywurst, and clean toilets you pay to use. The famous Sanifair coin is a rite of passage.
hiking trail
Wandern (to hike) + Weg (path). Germany has over 200,000 km\nof marked Wanderwege. Hiking is the national outdoor sport.

A: Is there a hiking trail here?
B: Yes, the trail leads through the forest.
A: How long is the hiking trail?
B: About twenty kilometers.
Wandern (to hike) + Weg (path). Germany has over 200,000 km of marked Wanderwege. Hiking is the national outdoor sport. Every region has its own long-distance Wanderweg.
stopover, intermediate stop
Zwischen (between) + Stopp (stop). An intermediate stop.\n'Wir machen einen Zwischenstopp in Heidelberg.'

A: Shall we make a stopover?
B: Yes, in Heidelberg.
A: How long will the stopover last?
B: One hour, then we drive on.
Zwischen (between) + Stopp (stop). An intermediate stop, a layover. 'Wir machen einen Zwischenstopp in Heidelberg.' The compound captures the between-ness perfectly.
to travel through, to tour
Be- + reisen (to travel). The be- prefix makes it transitive:\nyou 'bereisen ein Land.' More thorough than just 'reisen.'

A: Have you traveled through all of Germany?
B: Almost. I still need to see the north.
A: I want to tour southern Germany.
B: Start with the Black Forest!
Be- + reisen (to travel). The be- prefix makes it transitive: you 'bereisen ein Land' (travel through a country). More thorough than just 'reisen.' Bereisen implies you explore the place properly.
to traverse, to cross through
Durch (through) + queren (to cross). Not separable.\n'Wir durchqueren drei Bundesländer.' One continuous crossing.

A: How many federal states are we crossing?
B: Three: Hesse, Baden-Württemberg, and Bavaria.
A: Are we crossing the Black Forest too?
B: No, we are going around it.
Durch (through) + queren (to cross). To traverse, to cross through. Not separable because durch- is inseparable here. 'Wir durchqueren drei Bundesländer.' One continuous crossing.
to explore, to scout out
Er- + kunden (related to Kunde, knowledge). Military origin:\nErkundung was reconnaissance. Now means curious exploration.

A: Shall we explore the old town?
B: Yes, on foot!
A: I like exploring new cities.
B: Then Freiburg is perfect for you.

Er- + kunden (related to Kunde, knowledge). To explore, to scout out. Military origin: Erkundung was reconnaissance. Now it means any kind of curious exploration. 'Die Stadt erkunden' (to explore the city).
to get around, to travel widely
Herum (around) + kommen (to come). Separable. Colloquial.\n'Er ist viel herumgekommen' (He has traveled a lot).

A: Have you traveled around a lot?
B: Yes, I have been to ten countries.
A: You can get around well here.
B: True, the trains go everywhere.
Herum (around) + kommen (to come). To get around, to travel widely. Separable. 'Er ist viel herumgekommen' (He has traveled a lot). The image: you come around to many places.
to hike
Same root as English 'wander.' Germans take Wandern seriously:\nproper boots, trail maps, Wanderverein memberships.

A: Shall we go hiking tomorrow?
B: Yes, in the Black Forest.
A: How long will we hike?
B: Five hours, with breaks.
Same root as English 'wander.' Germans take Wandern seriously: proper boots, trail maps, Wanderverein (hiking club) memberships. 'Wer wandert, der findet' is an old saying.
to stay overnight
Über (over) + Nacht (night). Literally 'to go over a night.'\nNot separable. 'Wo übernachten wir?'

A: Where are we staying overnight in Freiburg?
B: In a youth hostel.
A: Have you ever stayed overnight in a tent?
B: Yes, at a campsite in the Black Forest.
Über (over) + Nacht (night). To stay overnight. The word literally means to 'go over a night.' Not separable. 'Wo übernachten wir?' is the first question on every road trip.
breathtaking
Atem (breath) + beraubend (robbing). Literally breath-robbing.\nOne of German's most dramatic adjectives. Three components in one.

A: The view is breathtaking!
B: Yes, you can see all the way to the Alps.
A: The valley is also breathtaking.
B: Freiburg simply has everything.
Atem (breath) + beraubend (robbing). Breathtaking, literally breath-robbing. 'Die Aussicht ist atemberaubend.' One of German's most dramatic adjectives. Three components welded into one word.
scenic, relating to landscape
Landschaft (landscape) + -lich (suffix). 'Landschaftlich schön'\nis how Germans describe their favorite drives.

A: Is the route scenic?
B: Yes, very. Mountains, forests, and lakes.
A: Scenically, the south is the best.
B: You are right about that.
Landschaft (landscape) + -lich (adjective suffix). Scenic, relating to landscape. 'Landschaftlich schön' (scenically beautiful) is how Germans describe their favorite drives.
picturesque
From Maler (painter) + -isch. Literally 'painter-like.'\nA place so beautiful a painter would stop to capture it.

A: Freiburg is truly picturesque.
B: Yes, especially the old town.
A: Do you know other picturesque cities?
B: Heidelberg and Rothenburg are also picturesque.

From Maler (painter) + -isch. Picturesque, literally 'painter-like.' A place so beautiful a painter would stop to capture it. 'Ein malerisches Dorf' (a picturesque village). The highest compliment for a landscape.
idyllic
From Greek 'eidyllion' (short pastoral poem). Same word\nacross European languages. Implies peace and rural charm.

A: The village looks idyllic.
B: Yes, totally quiet and peaceful.
A: An idyllic place to stay overnight.
B: Perfect for a break from everyday life.
From Greek 'eidyllion' (short pastoral poem). Idyllic. Same word across European languages. 'Ein idyllischer Ort' (an idyllic place). Implies peace, rural charm, and absence of stress.
afterward, after that
Da (there) + nach (after). A temporal adverb.\n'Zuerst Heidelberg, danach Freiburg.' Clean sequencing.

A: What do we do first?
B: First the old town, then the cathedral.
A: And after that?
B: After that we eat something.
Da (there) + nach (after). Afterward, after that. A temporal adverb. 'Zuerst Heidelberg, danach Freiburg.' Clean sequencing. Essential for storytelling and travel planning.
meanwhile, in the meantime
In + zwischen (between). Marks time passing between events.\n'Inzwischen hat sich viel verändert.' Elegant and common.

A: We have been driving for three hours.
B: Meanwhile, it has started to rain.
A: In the meantime, we are almost there.
B: Good, I am hungry!
In + zwischen (between). Meanwhile, in the meantime. 'Inzwischen hat sich viel verändert' (Meanwhile, a lot has changed). Marks time passing between events. Elegant and commonly used.
The largest is Bavaria, the smallest is Bremen. The number is between 15 and 17.
The verb with be- prefix meaning to travel thoroughly through a region.
We want to travel through all of southern Germany.
Master vocabulary for German landmarks and cultural sites. Describe cities and their historical features. Plan sightseeing routes.
Learn 18 words about landmarks and culture
Describe cities and historical sites
Plan sightseeing tours
old town
Alt (old) + Stadt (city). Every German city with medieval roots\nhas an Altstadt: narrow streets, half-timbered houses.

A: Have you seen the old town of Freiburg?
B: Yes, it is beautiful.
A: The old town has narrow lanes.
B: And water flows everywhere in the little streams.
Alt (old) + Stadt (city). Every German city with medieval roots has an Altstadt: narrow streets, half-timbered houses, a central Marktplatz. Freiburg's Altstadt survived WWII bombing mostly intact.
castle (fortified)
Same root as English 'borough' and French 'bourg.' A fortified\ncastle for military defense. Different from Schloss (palace).

A: Is that a castle or a palace?
B: A castle. See the thick walls?
A: The castle sits high on the hill.
B: Yes, for defense.

Same root as English 'borough' and French 'bourg.' A castle (fortified, military). Different from Schloss (palace, residential). Die Burg sits on hilltops for defense. Germany has over 25,000 Burgen and Schlösser.
cathedral
From Latin 'domus Dei' (house of God). Der Kölner Dom took\n632 years to complete (1248-1880). Masculine from Latin origin.

A: The Freiburg Cathedral is impressive.
B: Yes, the tower is 116 meters high.
A: Have you seen the Cologne Cathedral?
B: Yes, it is even bigger!
From Latin 'domus Dei' (house of God). A cathedral. Der Kölner Dom took 632 years to complete (1248-1880). Every major German city has its Dom. Masculine because of the Latin origin.
palace, stately home
From schließen (to close/lock). Originally a 'locked' building.\nSchloss Neuschwanstein inspired Disney's castle. Neuter.

A: Neuschwanstein Palace is world-famous.
B: Yes, the Disney palace!
A: Is there a palace in Freiburg?
B: Not directly, but several in the region.
From schließen (to close/lock). Originally a 'locked' building. Schloss Neuschwanstein inspired Disney's castle. Das Schloss is neuter. Distinction: Burg = fortress, Schloss = residence.
market square
Markt (market) + Platz (square). The Rathaus usually faces\nthe Marktplatz. Christmas markets set up here every December.

A: Shall we meet at the market square?
B: Yes, in front of the town hall.
A: The market square is full of people.
B: Today is market day!
Markt (market) + Platz (square). The central market square. Every traditional German town has one. The Rathaus (town hall) usually faces the Marktplatz. Christmas markets set up here every December.
city wall
Stadt (city) + Mauer (wall). Many German cities still have\nmedieval Stadtmauern: Rothenburg, Nürnberg, Lübeck.

A: Can you walk along the city wall?
B: Yes, in Rothenburg you can.
A: The city wall is over 500 years old.
B: And still sturdy!
Stadt (city) + Mauer (wall). A city wall. Many German cities still have sections of their medieval Stadtmauer: Rothenburg, Nürnberg, Lübeck. Walking along the Stadtmauer is a top tourist activity.
landmark, emblem
Wahr (true) + Zeichen (sign). Das Brandenburger Tor ist\nBerlins Wahrzeichen. Every city has one defining symbol.

A: What is Freiburg's landmark?
B: The Freiburg Minster.
A: And Berlin's landmark?
B: The Brandenburg Gate, of course.
Wahr (true) + Zeichen (sign). A landmark, an emblem. The Brandenburger Tor is Berlin's Wahrzeichen. Every city has one thing that defines it visually. Das Wahrzeichen is neuter.
tourist attraction, sight
Sehen (see) + würdig (worthy) + -keit (suffix). One of German's\nlongest everyday compounds. Worth seeing.

A: What are the top sights in Freiburg?
B: The cathedral, the little streams, and the castle hill.
A: Are there many sights?
B: Yes, you need at least two days.

Sehen (see) + würdig (worthy) + -keit (noun suffix). A sight worth seeing, a tourist attraction. One of German's longest everyday compounds. 'Die Top-10-Sehenswürdigkeiten' appears on every travel website.
vineyard
Wein (wine) + Berg (mountain/hill). Germany has 13 wine regions.\nMasculine because Berg is masculine.

A: Do you see the vineyards?
B: Yes, they are all over the hills.
A: Freiburg lies in the middle of vineyards.
B: That is why the wine here is so good.
Wein (wine) + Berg (mountain/hill). A vineyard. Germany has 13 wine regions. The steep Weinberge along the Mosel and Rhine produce world-class Riesling. Masculine because Berg is masculine.
outdoor swimming pool
Frei (open/free) + Bad (bath/pool). Germans love their Freibäder\nin summer. The season runs May to September. Neuter.

A: Shall we go to the outdoor pool?
B: Yes, perfect in this weather!
A: Freiburg has a nice outdoor pool.
B: With a view of the mountains!
Frei (open/free) + Bad (bath/pool). An outdoor swimming pool. Germans love their Freibäder in summer. Every town has one. The Freibad season runs May to September. Neuter because Bad is neuter.
city tour
Stadt (city) + Führung (guided tour). Feminine because Führung\nis feminine. Free walking tours are popular in every German city.

A: Shall we take a city tour?
B: Yes, good idea!
A: The city tour lasts two hours.
B: Perfect, then we will see everything.
Stadt (city) + Führung (guided tour). A city tour. 'Eine Stadtführung machen' (to take a city tour). Feminine because Führung is feminine. Free walking tours are popular in every German city.
historical
From Greek 'historia.' Same word across European languages.\nGermans are meticulous about preserving historische Bausubstanz.

A: Is that a historical building?
B: Yes, from the 14th century.
A: The historical old town is protected.
B: Yes, you are not allowed to change anything.

From Greek 'historia.' Historical. Same word across European languages. 'Ein historisches Gebäude' (a historical building). Germans are meticulous about preserving historische Bausubstanz (historical building fabric).
medieval
Mittelalter (Middle Ages) + -lich. The Mittelalter lasted roughly\nfrom 500-1500 AD. Many Altstädte still look mittelalterlich.

A: This castle is medieval.
B: Yes, from the 12th century.
A: The medieval lanes are narrow.
B: But beautiful.

Mittelalter (Middle Ages) + -lich. Medieval. 'Eine mittelalterliche Burg' (a medieval castle). The Mittelalter lasted roughly from 500-1500 AD. Many German cities still look mittelalterlich in their Altstadt.
worth seeing
Sehen (to see) + wert (worth). Shorter than Sehenswürdigkeit\nbut same root. A practical adjective for travel tips.

A: Is Freiburg worth seeing?
B: Absolutely!
A: What is especially worth seeing?
B: The cathedral and the little streams.

Sehen (to see) + wert (worth). Worth seeing. Shorter than Sehenswürdigkeit but same root. 'Die Altstadt ist sehenswert' (The old town is worth seeing). A practical adjective for travel recommendations.
charming, attractive
Reiz (charm/stimulus) + voll (full). More subtle than 'schön.'\nImplies something that draws you in gently.

A: The landscape here is charming.
B: Yes, the hills and vineyards.
A: A charming village!
B: Perfect for a stopover.
Reiz (charm/stimulus) + voll (full). Charming, attractive, full of appeal. 'Eine reizvolle Landschaft' (a charming landscape). More subtle than 'schön.' Implies something that draws you in gently.
picturesque (formal)
From French 'pittoresque,' from Italian 'pittoresco.'\nSame meaning as 'malerisch' but more formal and literary.

A: The village is truly picturesque.
B: Yes, like out of a painting.
A: 'Pittoresk' sounds very elegant.
B: It comes from French.

From French 'pittoresque,' from Italian 'pittoresco' (painter-like). Picturesque. Same meaning as 'malerisch' but with a French flavor. Used in formal writing and travel literature. An elegant synonym.
impressive
Eindruck (impression) + voll (full). Full of impression.\n'Ein eindrucksvolles Bauwerk.' The opposite of 'unscheinbar.'

A: The cathedral is truly impressive.
B: Yes, especially from the inside.
A: An impressive structure.
B: You feel very small in front of it.
Eindruck (impression) + voll (full). Impressive, full of impression. 'Ein eindrucksvolles Bauwerk' (an impressive structure). The opposite of 'unscheinbar' (inconspicuous).
to visit, to tour (a place)
Be- + sichtigen (from Sicht, sight). The be- prefix makes it\ntransitive. Implies a thorough, deliberate look.

A: Shall we visit the palace?
B: Yes, the tour starts at ten.
A: Can you visit the cathedral too?
B: Yes, admission is free.

Be- + sichtigen (from Sicht, sight). To visit, to tour, to inspect. 'Das Schloss besichtigen' (to visit the castle). The be- prefix makes it transitive: you besichtigen a place. Implies a thorough look.
One stands on hilltops for defense. The other was the residence of the nobility.
The formal verb with be- prefix for a thorough visit to a place.
Tomorrow we want to visit the palace.
A place so beautiful that a painter would want to capture it.
A small village with half-timbered houses by a river. Which adjective fits best?
A medieval old town, an impressive cathedral, and many places worth seeing.
Freiburg has a medieval old town with an impressive cathedral and many sights.
Wahr (true) + Zeichen (sign). Think of the Brandenburger Tor in Berlin.
Learn the three negation prefixes that darken German words. Understand regional dialects and linguistic diversity. Play with word formation.
Learn the prefixes un-, miss-, and zer-
Understand regional dialects and language variation
Play with word formation and chain words
The A5. What started as open-road freedom is now a solid wall of red brake lights. Traffic jam. The eternal Autobahn experience.
Traffic jam. Of course. Always before Karlsruhe. Shall we play a game?
Chain word! Everyone says a word that starts with the last part. I will start: Kuchen (cake).
Kuchentisch (cake table).
Tischdecke (tablecloth).
Deckenlampe (ceiling lamp).
Lampenfieber (stage fright)!
Lampenfieber! That is a beautiful word! Verumius wins this round.
to misunderstand
Miss- (wrongly) + verstehen (to understand). Not separable.\n'Da hast du mich missverstanden.' Related to English 'mis-.'

A: You misunderstood me.
B: Sorry! What do you mean?
A: Misunderstandings happen often.
B: Especially in a foreign language.

Miss- (wrongly) + verstehen (to understand). To misunderstand. 'Da hast du mich missverstanden.' The miss- prefix means something went wrong. Not separable. Related to English 'mis-' (misunderstand, mislead).
to destroy
Zer- (apart, to pieces) + stören (to disturb). The most violent\nprefix. Zerbrechen, zerreißen, zerfallen. Destruction is always zer-.

A: The storm destroyed the roof.
B: How terrible.
A: Can it be repaired?
B: Yes, but it takes weeks.

Zer- (apart, to pieces) + stören (to disturb). To destroy. The zer- prefix is the most violent: it means to break into pieces. Zerbrechen (break apart), zerreißen (tear apart), zerfallen (fall apart).
dialect (literary term)
Mund (mouth) + Art (way/type). Literally 'the way of the mouth.'\nMore literary than Dialekt. Feminine.

A: Do you speak a dialect?
B: Yes, Swabian.
A: The Bavarian dialect sounds completely different.
B: Yes, like its own language.

Mund (mouth) + Art (way/type). Dialect, literally 'the way of the mouth.' A more literary/academic term than Dialekt. 'Die bayerische Mundart' (the Bavarian dialect). Every region has its own Mundart.
Standard German
Hoch (high) + Deutsch. Originally the language of the southern\nhighlands. Now the prestige dialect taught in schools and on TV.

A: Do you speak Standard German?
B: Yes, but with a slight accent.
A: On TV you only hear Standard German.
B: Yes, dialect is rare on TV.
Hoch (high) + Deutsch. Originally referred to the language of the southern highlands (where German consonant shifts happened). Now means the prestige dialect taught in schools and used on TV.
colloquial language
Umgang (interaction) + Sprache (language). Between Hochdeutsch\nand Dialekt. The relaxed German people actually speak.

A: 'Cool' is colloquial language.
B: Yes, not Standard German.
A: You do not learn colloquial language from a book.
B: No, only in real life.

Umgang (interaction) + Sprache (language). Colloquial language, everyday speech. Between Hochdeutsch and Dialekt sits Umgangssprache: the relaxed German people actually speak. Regional color without full dialect.
native speaker
Muttersprache (mother tongue) + -ler (person). The -ler suffix\nturns a concept into the person who embodies it.

A: Are you a native speaker?
B: Yes, German is my mother tongue.
A: Verumius wants to sound like a native speaker.
B: He will make it!
Muttersprache (mother tongue) + -ler (person). A native speaker. 'Er spricht wie ein Muttersprachler.' The -ler suffix turns a concept into the person who embodies it.
dialect
From Greek 'dialektos' (manner of speaking). Germany has dozens:\nBayerisch, Schwäbisch, Sächsisch, Kölsch, Plattdeutsch.

A: Which dialect do they speak here?
B: Swabian.
A: Some dialects need subtitles on TV.
B: Yes, especially Saxon!

From Greek 'dialektos' (manner of speaking). A regional variety of a language. Germany has dozens: Bayerisch, Schwäbisch, Sächsisch, Kölsch, Plattdeutsch. Some are so different they need subtitles on TV.
Low German
Platt (flat/low) + Deutsch. Spoken in northern Germany's flat\nlowlands. Closer to Dutch and English than to Hochdeutsch.

A: Do you speak Low German?
B: Just a little bit. Moin!
A: 'Moin' is Low German?
B: Yes, the most famous word.

Platt (flat/low) + Deutsch. Low German. Spoken in northern Germany's flat lowlands. A separate language branch, closer to Dutch and English than to Hochdeutsch. 'Moin!' (Hello!) is the most famous Plattdeutsch word.
Swabian (dialect)
The dialect of Swabia (Baden-Württemberg). Famous for 'schaffen'\n(to work hard) and 'Kehrwoche' (weekly cleaning rotation).

A: Do you understand Swabian?
B: Hardly! It sounds so different.
A: Swabian has its own grammar.
B: Yes, almost its own language.

The dialect of Swabia (Baden-Württemberg). Famous for 'schaffen' (to work hard) and 'Kehrwoche' (weekly cleaning rotation). Swabian speakers are stereotyped as thrifty, hardworking, and proud of their dialect.
northern German
Nord (north) + deutsch. Norddeutsch culture is flatter, dryer\n(in humor and landscape), and more reserved.

A: Northern German food is different from southern.
B: Yes, more fish and fewer dumplings.
A: Northern Germans are more reserved.
B: But very friendly once you know them.

Nord (north) + deutsch. Northern German. Norddeutsch culture is flatter, dryer (in humor and landscape), and more reserved. 'Moin' (morning greeting at any time of day) is quintessentially norddeutsch.
southern German
Süd (south) + deutsch. Süddeutsche Regionen haben stärkere\nDialekte, katholische Traditionen und anderes Essen.

A: Southern Germany is completely different from the north.
B: Yes, different dialects, different food.
A: Southern German Spätzle are fantastic.
B: And pretzels even more so!
Süd (south) + deutsch. Southern German. Süddeutsch regions (Bayern, Baden-Württemberg) have stronger dialects, Catholic traditions, and different food (Spätzle, Weißwurst, Brezeln).
Bavarian
From Bayern (Bavaria). The most famous German dialect\ninternationally, thanks to Oktoberfest.

A: Do you speak Bavarian?
B: A little! Servus!
A: 'Servus' is Bavarian for hello?
B: Yes, and also for goodbye.
From Bayern (Bavaria). The most famous German dialect internationally, thanks to Oktoberfest. 'Servus' (hello/goodbye), 'Grüß Gott' (greeting), 'Oans, zwoa, drei' (one, two, three).
original, originally
Ursprung (origin) + -lich. Three syllables packed with meaning.\n'Ursprünglich wollte ich nach München.'

A: Where does the word originally come from?
B: From Middle High German.
A: Originally we wanted to go to Munich.
B: But Freiburg is better!
Ursprung (origin) + -lich. Original, originally, in its original form. 'Ursprünglich wollte ich nach München' (Originally I wanted to go to Munich). Three syllables packed with meaning.
old-fashioned, archaic
Altertum (antiquity) + -lich. Describes language or customs\nthat feel like they come from another century.

A: This word sounds archaic.
B: Yes, nobody uses it anymore.
A: The dialect sometimes sounds archaic.
B: Yes, it has preserved old forms.
Altertum (antiquity) + -lich. Old-fashioned, archaic. Describes language or customs that feel like they come from another century. Not 'alt' (old), but 'from ancient times.'
simultaneous, at the same time
Gleich (same) + zeitig (related to time). A temporal adverb.\nElegant way to describe parallel events.

A: Can you drive and eat at the same time?
B: Better not!
A: It is raining and the sun is shining at the same time.
B: Typical for the Black Forest!
Gleich (same) + zeitig (related to time). Simultaneous, at the same time. 'Gleichzeitig regnet es und scheint die Sonne.' Elegant way to describe parallel events.
meanwhile (literary)
Unter (under/during) + dessen (of that). More literary than\n'inzwischen.' Sets scenes beautifully in narrative.

A: Lukas drove on.
B: Meanwhile, Hildi fell asleep.
A: Meanwhile, it got dark.
B: And the traffic jam cleared.
Unter (under/during) + dessen (of that). Meanwhile, in the meantime. More literary than 'inzwischen.' 'Unterdessen spielten sie ein Wortspiel.' Sets scenes beautifully in narrative.
because of this, therefore
Des (of this) + wegen (because of). A kausal adverb.\n'Es regnete, deswegen blieben wir im Auto.' Same as 'deshalb.'

A: It is raining. That is why we are staying in the car.
B: Good idea.
A: The traffic jam is long. That is why we are playing.
B: That is why I love word games!
Des (of this) + wegen (because of). Because of this, therefore. A causal adverb. 'Es regnete, deswegen blieben wir im Auto.' Cause and effect in one word. Same meaning as 'deshalb.'
therefore, for that reason
Des (of this) + halb (half/behalf). Interchangeable with\n'deswegen.' The causal bridge between two facts.

A: I love languages, that is why I am learning German.
B: That is a good reason!
A: Freiburg is beautiful, that is why we are going there.
B: That is why I am so excited!
Des (of this) + halb (half/behalf). Therefore, for that reason. Interchangeable with 'deswegen.' 'Ich liebe Sprachen, deshalb lerne ich Deutsch.' The causal bridge between two facts.

Three prefixes negate or intensify words in different ways. All three are inseparable.

- un- creates mild opposites: unglücklich (unhappy), unmöglich (impossible), unfreundlich (unfriendly). It works just like English 'un-'.
- miss- signals something done wrongly or a failure: missverstehen (to misunderstand), Misserfolg (failure), misstrauen (to distrust).
- zer- is the most violent, meaning 'to pieces' or 'apart': zerstören (to destroy), zerbrechen (to shatter), zerreißen (to tear apart).

The scale runs from mild reversal (un-) through error (miss-) to violent destruction (zer-).

Un- is the mildest negation prefix. It creates opposites, just like English 'un-': happy/unhappy, friendly/unfriendly. Some un- words have shifted meaning: 'Ungeheuer' originally meant 'un-homely' but now means 'monster.'

Miss- indicates something done wrongly or a failure. The prefix is always unstressed. 'Missverständnis' (misunderstanding), 'Misserfolg' (failure), 'Missbrauch' (misuse/abuse). English 'mis-' works the same way.

Zer- is the most destructive. It means 'to pieces' or 'apart.' Every zer- verb implies violent fragmentation: zerbrechen (break to pieces), zerreißen (tear apart), zerfallen (fall apart), zerschlagen (smash to pieces). Zer- is always inseparable.
The most destructive prefix. 'Zerbrechen,' 'zerreißen,' 'zerfallen.' Everything falls apart.
He understood me incorrectly. The prefix for 'done wrong' plus 'verstehen.'
He completely misunderstood me.
From mild misfortune to a mistake to total destruction. The prefixes escalate.
Understand how German builds words from parts. Master the rules for compound nouns. Learn productive suffixes. Decode any long German word.
Understand word formation: Bestimmungswort + Grundwort
Learn productive suffixes (-ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft, -tum)
Decode any long German word through decomposition
word formation
Wort (word) + Bildung (formation). The science of how German\ncreates new words. Just stick words together, no limits.

A: What is word formation?
B: How you make new words from old ones.
A: German is the master of word formation.
B: Yes, you can build infinitely long words.

Wort (word) + Bildung (formation/education). Word formation. The science of how German creates new words. German is the champion of Wortbildung: just stick words together. No hyphens needed. No limits on length.
determining word (first part of compound)
Bestimmung (determination) + Wort (word). The first part of a\ncompound that specifies meaning. In 'Haustür,' 'Haus' is it.

A: What is the determining word in 'Haustier' (pet)?
B: 'Haus.' It determines the type of animal.
A: And in 'Autobahn'?
B: 'Auto.' It says what kind of road.
Bestimmung (determination) + Wort (word). The first part of a compound that specifies the meaning. In 'Haustür' (house door), 'Haus' is the Bestimmungswort. It narrows the meaning of the second part.
base word (last part of compound)
Grund (base/ground) + Wort (word). Always the last part.\nDetermines gender and basic category. Grundwort = boss.

A: What is the base word in 'Haustür' (house door)?
B: 'Tür.' It determines the gender: die Tür.
A: That is why it is 'die Haustür'?
B: Exactly! The base word is the boss.

Grund (base/ground) + Wort (word). The base word in a compound, always the last part. In 'Haustür,' 'Tür' is the Grundwort. It determines the gender and the basic category. Grundwort = boss of the compound.
compound word
Zusammen (together) + Setzung (placing). Germany's most famous:\n'Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän.'

A: What is a compound word?
B: A word made from two or more parts.
A: 'Bundesland' is a compound?
B: Yes! Bund + es + Land.
Zusammen (together) + Setzung (placing). A compound word. 'Donaudampfschifffahrtsgesellschaftskapitän' is Germany's most famous Zusammensetzung. The noun form of 'zusammensetzen.'
vocabulary
Wort (word) + Schatz (treasure). Literally 'word treasure.'\nMasculine because Schatz is masculine.

A: How large is your vocabulary?
B: It grows every day.
A: Having a large vocabulary is important.
B: Yes, it is a true treasure.

Wort (word) + Schatz (treasure). Vocabulary, literally 'word treasure.' Your Wortschatz is your treasure chest of known words. 'Einen großen Wortschatz haben' (to have a large vocabulary). Masculine because Schatz is masculine.
to derive
Ab (from/off) + leiten (to lead/guide). Separable.\n'Das Wort leitet sich vom Lateinischen ab.'

A: Where does this word derive from?
B: From Latin.
A: Can you derive all words?
B: Most have an origin.
Ab (from/off) + leiten (to lead/guide). To derive. 'Das Wort leitet sich vom Lateinischen ab.' Separable. The linguistic term for tracing a word back to its origin.
to put together, to compose
Zusammen (together) + setzen (to set/place). Separable.\nThe verb for German's favorite hobby: making compounds.

A: Let us put two words together!
B: Good. House and door.
A: House door! Done.
B: Putting words together is easy!
Zusammen (together) + setzen (to set/place). To put together, to compose. 'Wir setzen zwei Wörter zusammen' (We put two words together). Separable. German's favorite hobby: making compounds.
to divide up, to split
Auf (up/apart) + teilen (to divide). Separable.\nThe opposite of zusammensetzen. Deconstruction.

A: Let us split the word up.
B: 'Wanderweg': hiking + path.
A: Good! And 'Bundesland'?
B: Federation + linking-s + state. Easy!
Auf (up/apart) + teilen (to divide). To divide up, to split. 'Das Wort in seine Teile aufteilen' (to divide the word into its parts). The opposite of zusammensetzen. Separable.
settlement, housing estate
From siedeln (to settle). The -ung suffix makes it feminine.\nGermany has Siedlungen from every era: medieval to modern.

A: What is a settlement?
B: A place where people settle.
A: This settlement is from the 1950s.
B: Yes, typical of the economic miracle.
From siedeln (to settle). A settlement, a housing estate. Germany has Siedlungen from every era: medieval, Gründerzeit, postwar. The -ung suffix makes it feminine.
cultural heritage
Kultur (culture) + Erbe (heritage). Neuter because Erbe\n(heritage) is neuter. Germany has 52 UNESCO sites.

A: Is the old town cultural heritage?
B: Yes, it is under protection.
A: Cultural heritage must be preserved.
B: That is why you cannot demolish anything.
Kultur (culture) + Erbe (heritage). Cultural heritage. 'Immaterielles Kulturerbe' (intangible cultural heritage). Germany has 52 UNESCO Welterbe sites. Neuter because Erbe (heritage) is neuter.
Founders' Period (1871-1914)
Gründer (founder) + Zeit (time). An era of rapid industrialization\nand ornate architecture. Feminine because Zeit is feminine.

A: What is the Gründerzeit?
B: An era from 1871 to 1914.
A: Gründerzeit buildings are beautiful.
B: Yes, with decorated facades.

Gründer (founder) + Zeit (time). The Founders' Period (1871-1914). An era of rapid industrialization and ornate architecture in Germany. Gründerzeit buildings with their decorated facades define many German city centers.
Economic Miracle
Wirtschaft (economy) + Wunder (miracle). Germany's stunning\npostwar recovery from 1948-1968. Untranslatable.

A: What was the Economic Miracle?
B: Germany's recovery after the war.
A: From rubble to Europe's largest economy.
B: In just twenty years!

Wirtschaft (economy) + Wunder (miracle). The Economic Miracle. Germany's stunning postwar recovery from 1948-1968. From rubble to Europe's largest economy. The word captures national pride and collective effort.
structure, edifice
Bau (building/construction) + Werk (work). Grander than\n'Gebäude.' Der Kölner Dom is a Bauwerk, your garage is a Gebäude.

A: The cathedral is an impressive structure.
B: Yes, a masterpiece of Gothic architecture.
A: Which structure is the oldest in Freiburg?
B: Parts of the city wall from the 13th century.
Bau (building/construction) + Werk (work). A structure, an edifice. Grander than 'Gebäude' (building). Der Kölner Dom is a Bauwerk. Your garage is a Gebäude.
steeped in history
Geschichte (history) + trächtig (pregnant/laden). Literally\n'pregnant with history.' One of German's most evocative compounds.

A: Freiburg is a place steeped in history.
B: Yes, over 900 years old.
A: Every street is steeped in history.
B: You can feel the history everywhere.
Geschichte (history) + trächtig (pregnant/laden with). Steeped in history, historically significant. Literally 'pregnant with history.' One of German's most evocative compound adjectives.
typical of the region
Regional + typisch (typical). Describes anything authentically\nlocal. 'Regionaltypische Küche' (regional cuisine).

A: What is typical of the region in Freiburg?
B: Spätzle, Black Forest cake, wine.
A: Regional food is the best.
B: Yes, always fresh and local.
Regional + typisch (typical). Typical of the region, regionally characteristic. 'Regionaltypische Küche' (regional cuisine). A compound adjective that describes anything authentically local.
tradition-conscious
Tradition + bewusst (conscious/aware). Mindful of tradition.\nGermans value Traditionsbewusstsein alongside innovation.

A: Is Freiburg tradition-conscious?
B: Yes, very. The old town is well-maintained.
A: Tradition-conscious villages are charming.
B: Yes, they preserve their identity.

Tradition + bewusst (conscious/aware). Tradition-conscious, mindful of tradition. 'Ein traditionsbewusstes Dorf' (a village mindful of its traditions). Germans value Traditionsbewusstsein alongside innovation.
therefore, from there
Da (there) + her (from). Both causal ('Daher ist es wichtig')\nand directional ('Er kommt von daher'). Context decides.

A: Freiburg lies in the south. Therefore it is warm.
B: Yes, that is also where the vineyards come from.
A: That is why the city is so popular.
B: Warm, beautiful, and full of culture.
Da (there) + her (from). Therefore, from there. Both a causal adverb ('Daher ist es wichtig') and a directional one ('Er kommt von daher'). Context determines meaning. Same as 'deshalb' when causal.
A modal adverb. 'Ich wandere gern' (I like to hike).\n'Gern geschehen' (You're welcome, literally 'gladly done').

A: Do you like to hike?
B: Yes, very much!
A: You're welcome!
B: Thanks, you are very kind.
'Gladly, with pleasure.' 'Ich wandere gern' (I like to hike). 'Gern geschehen' (You're welcome, literally 'gladly done'). One of the most-used small words in German.

German builds words by combining parts in predictable ways.

Compound nouns join a Bestimmungswort (specifier) and a Grundwort (base). The Grundwort is always last and determines gender: 'Haus' plus 'Tür' gives 'Haustür,' and since Tür is feminine, so is Haustür.

Sometimes a linking element (Fugenelement) appears between the parts: Arbeit-s-platz, Freundschaft-s-ring, Bundesland.

Noun-forming suffixes:
- -ung creates feminine nouns: Bildung, Siedlung
- -heit and -keit create feminine abstract nouns: Freiheit, Möglichkeit
- -schaft creates feminine collective nouns: Freundschaft
- -tum creates neuter nouns: Wachstum, Altertum

Adjective-forming suffixes:
- -lich: freundlich, landschaftlich
- -bar: machbar, trinkbar
- -ig: traurig, giftig
- -sam: sparsam, einsam

Linking elements (Fugenelemente) follow patterns but have exceptions. The most common is -s-: Arbeit-s-platz, Geburt-s-tag, Wirtschaft-s-wunder. Feminine nouns with -ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft, -tion almost always take -s-.

Other linking elements: -n- (Blume-n-vase), -en- (Frau-en-haus), -er- (Kind-er-garten), -e- (Hund-e-hütte).

The Grundwort always wins for gender. 'Die Haustür' (feminine, because Tür is feminine). 'Der Wanderweg' (masculine, because Weg is masculine). This rule has no exceptions.

Long compounds are read right-to-left: identify the Grundwort first, then add the Bestimmungswörter as modifiers.

The strategy for decoding long German words is to read from right to left.

The Grundwort (far right) gives the category and gender. Everything to its left specifies what kind.

Take 'Bundestagsabgeordneter': the Grundwort is Abgeordneter (delegate), and Bundestag (federal parliament) specifies which kind. Together: a member of the federal parliament. You already know both halves. The compound is just the packaging.

Another example: 'Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung.' The Grundwort is Begrenzung (limit), and Geschwindigkeit (speed) tells you what is being limited. Result: speed limit.
The base word is the boss. It determines gender and core meaning.
This suffix forms feminine abstract nouns from adjectives ending in -lich or -ig.
'Möglichkeit' comes from 'möglich' plus the ending -keit.
Some words join directly without a linking element. Here the two parts simply meet.
laden with, full of, pregnant (animals)
Often used in compounds: geschichtsträchtig (steeped in history), zukunftsträchtig (promising for the future).
This city is steeped in history.
For humans, German uses schwanger. The word trächtig is reserved for animals and figurative compounds.
Decompose the compound adjective into its two elements. The second element comes from a word meaning laden or pregnant with something. Both elements appear among the pool options.
Geschichtsträchtig = Geschichte + trächtig. It means: full of history.
Master German sentence order for adverbs. Learn the TeKaMoLo rule. Explore Freiburg's culture and traditions.
Learn the TeKaMoLo rule for adverb order
Discover Freiburg's culture and traditions
Combine all three grammar constructs

Freiburg. The old town opens before them like a storybook. Narrow cobblestone streets. The famous Bächle (tiny water channels) run along the gutters. Half-timbered houses lean against each other. The cathedral tower rises above everything.
The Bächle! Whoever steps in must marry a Freiburger. Careful, Verumius!
Then I will be careful. I want to explore the city first, before I get married.
Verumius, in German there is an order for adverbs: first WHEN, then WHY, then HOW, then WHERE.
An order? For adverbs?
TeKaMoLo. Temporal, Causal, Modal, Local. 'I gladly drive to Freiburg tomorrow because of work.' When, why, how, where.
nature reserve
Natur (nature) + Schutz (protection) + Gebiet (area).\nThree nouns welded into one protected area.

A: Is that a nature reserve?
B: Yes, the Black Forest has many of them.
A: Can you hike there?
B: Yes, but only on the marked trails.

Natur (nature) + Schutz (protection) + Gebiet (area). Germany has over 8,700 Naturschutzgebiete covering 6.3% of the country. The Black Forest near Freiburg contains several. Three nouns, one protected area.
half-timbered construction
Fach (compartment) + Werk (work). The iconic German\nbuilding style with visible wooden frames.

A: What is Fachwerk?
B: A building style with visible wooden beams.
A: Is there a lot of Fachwerk in Freiburg?
B: Yes, the whole old town is full of it.

Fach (compartment/panel) + Werk (work). Fachwerkhäuser are the iconic German buildings with visible wooden frames and white plaster between. Freiburg, Rothenburg, and Quedlinburg are Fachwerk capitals.
municipality, community
From 'gemein' (common/shared). The smallest\nadministrative unit in Germany.

A: How big is this municipality?
B: About 5,000 residents.
A: Does every municipality have a mayor?
B: Yes, that is mandatory.
From 'gemein' (common/shared). Die Gemeinde is the smallest administrative unit in Germany. Every village is a Gemeinde. Also means a church congregation.
the Hanseatic League
From Middle Low German 'hanse' (group of merchants).\nA medieval trade alliance of northern German cities.

A: What was the Hanse?
B: An alliance of northern German merchants.
A: Which cities belonged to it?
B: Hamburg, Bremen, Lübeck, Rostock.
From Middle Low German 'hanse' (group of merchants). The Hanseatic League dominated Baltic trade for centuries. Hamburg still calls itself 'Freie und Hansestadt Hamburg.'
cultural event
Kultur (culture) + Veranstaltung (event). Concerts,\ntheater, exhibitions. A transparent compound.

A: Is there a cultural event today?
B: Yes, a concert at the Münsterplatz.
A: Freiburg has many cultural events.
B: Almost every evening something happens.
Kultur (culture) + Veranstaltung (event). Concerts, theater, exhibitions. 'Freiburg hat viele Kulturveranstaltungen.' The compound is long but transparent: a culture + event.
regulars' table at a pub
Stamm (trunk/regular) + Tisch (table). A reserved table\nwhere the same group meets weekly.

A: What is a Stammtisch?
B: A table for the regulars.
A: Do you have a Stammtisch?
B: Yes, every Thursday at the pub.

Stamm (trunk/regular) + Tisch (table). A reserved table where the same group meets weekly. Stammtisch culture is quintessentially German: beer, discussion, community. Some Stammtische have existed for generations.
folk festival
Volk (people) + Fest (festival). Oktoberfest is the\nmost famous, but every region has its own.

A: Have you been to a folk festival?
B: Yes, to the Munich Oktoberfest.
A: That is the biggest folk festival in the world.
B: Millions of visitors every year.
Volk (people) + Fest (festival). Oktoberfest is the most famous Volksfest, but every region has its own: Cannstatter Wasen, Hamburger Dom, Dürkheimer Wurstmarkt. Neuter because Fest is neuter.
fair, annual market
Jahr (year) + Markt (market). Originally a yearly\ntrading event. Now: rides, cotton candy, games.

A: Shall we go to the fair?
B: Yes, I love fairs!
A: Ferris wheel or roller coaster?
B: First ferris wheel, then roller coaster.
Jahr (year) + Markt (market). Originally a yearly trading event in medieval towns. Now: rides, cotton candy, carnival games. Different from a Volksfest (which focuses on beer and tradition).
carnival (southern German term)
From Middle High German 'vastschang' (the last drink\nbefore Lent). Same as Karneval, different name.

A: Do you celebrate Fasching?
B: Yes, in southern Germany we say Fasching.
A: And in the Rhineland?
B: There it is called Karneval.

From Middle High German 'vastschang' (the last drink before Lent). Called Fasching in southern Germany, Karneval in the Rhineland, and Fastnacht in southwestern Germany. Same celebration, three names.
to discover
Ent- (un-) + decken (to cover). To uncover, to discover.\nNot separable. Columbus entdeckte Amerika.

A: What did you discover in Freiburg?
B: A great café in the old town.
A: I like to discover new places.
B: Me too. Discovering makes you happy.
Ent- (un-) + decken (to cover). To discover, literally to uncover. Columbus 'entdeckte' America. You 'entdecken' a hidden café. The ent- prefix reverses the covering. Not separable.
Stamm (regular/core group) + Tisch. The same group gathers here every week for community.
Strolling, stopping by, exploring: the verbs of regional travel. Plus adverbs of possibility and the warm feeling of Gemuetlichkeit.
Learn verbs for local exploration
Use adverbs of possibility
Describe places with atmosphere
to stroll leisurely
From French 'flâner' (to stroll). A civilized,\nunhurried walk through a beautiful place.

A: Shall we stroll through the old town?
B: Yes, perfect in this weather.
A: Strolling is better than rushing.
B: Completely relaxed, without hurry.
From French 'flâner' (to stroll). 'Durch die Altstadt flanieren' (to stroll through the old town). Borrowed from French with the original meaning intact. A civilized, unhurried walk.
to stop in at an inn
Ein (in) + kehren (to turn). To stop in for food\nand drink. Separable. From the hiking tradition.

A: Shall we stop in somewhere?
B: Yes, at that inn over there.
A: After hiking you like to stop in.
B: A beer and a pretzel, please!
Ein (in) + kehren (to turn). To stop in at an inn or restaurant. Separable. A word from the hiking tradition: after a long walk, you einkehren for food and drink.
to drop by, to stop in briefly
Vorbei (past/by) + schauen (to look). Separable.\nMore casual than 'besuchen.' South German style.

A: Drop by sometime!
B: Gladly, when suits you?
A: This afternoon?
B: Perfect, I will drop by at three.
Vorbei (past/by) + schauen (to look). To drop by, to stop in briefly. Separable. 'Schau mal vorbei!' (Drop by sometime!). More casual than 'besuchen.' The South German version of 'vorbeikommen.'
cosmopolitan
From Greek 'kosmopolites' (citizen of the world).\nSame word internationally. Freiburg is surprisingly so.

A: Is Freiburg cosmopolitan?
B: Yes, because of the university.
A: Students from all over the world.
B: And the proximity to France and Switzerland.
From Greek 'kosmopolites' (citizen of the world). Cosmopolitan. Freiburg, despite its size, is surprisingly kosmopolitisch thanks to its university and proximity to France and Switzerland.
remote, secluded
Ab (away) + gelegen (situated). Past participle of\n'abliegen' used as adjective. Far from everything.

A: The village is quite remote.
B: Yes, no highway, no train station.
A: But the landscape is wonderful.
B: Remote, but breathtaking.
Ab (away) + gelegen (situated). 'Ein abgelegenes Dorf im Schwarzwald' (a remote village in the Black Forest). The past participle of 'abliegen' used as an adjective. Far away from everything.
somehow
Irgend (some-) + wie (how). A modal adverb.\nPart of the irgend- family: -wann, -wo, -wie.

A: Somehow we will manage.
B: Are you sure?
A: Yes, somehow it always works out.
B: Your optimism is contagious.
Irgend (some-) + wie (how). Somehow. A modal adverb. 'Irgendwie schaffen wir das.' The 'irgend-' family: irgendwann (sometime), irgendwo (somewhere), irgendwie (somehow).
possibly, perhaps
Möglich (possible) + er + weise (way/manner). A formal\nmodal adverb. More formal than 'vielleicht.'

A: Will it rain tomorrow?
B: Possibly, according to the weather report.
A: Then we will take umbrellas.
B: Possibly we won't need them.
Möglich (possible) + er + weise (way/manner). Possibly, perhaps. A formal modal adverb. More formal than 'vielleicht.' The -erweise ending signals manner.
presumably, probably
From 'vermuten' (to suspect/assume). Expresses educated\nguessing. Stronger than 'vielleicht.'

A: Will Lukas arrive on time?
B: Presumably not, he is always late.
A: Presumably he got lost.
B: That would not be the first time.
From 'vermuten' (to suspect/assume). Presumably, probably. 'Vermutlich kommt er später.' Expresses educated guessing. Stronger than 'vielleicht' (maybe) but weaker than 'wahrscheinlich' (likely).
curiosity
Feminine noun. Also 'die Neugier' or 'die Neugierigkeit.'\n'Aus Neugier' (out of curiosity) is a fixed phrase.

A: Why did you go to Freiburg?
B: Out of curiosity. I wanted to see the city.
A: Curiosity is a good quality.
B: Yes, it opens new worlds.

From 'neu' (new) + 'gier' (greed/desire). Literally 'new-desire.' The same root as 'gierig' (greedy). 'Aus Neugier' (out of curiosity) is a set phrase that marks the Ka (Kausal) slot in TeKaMoLo. In German, being curious about something is a virtue, not a flaw.
cozy, comfortable, leisurely
From 'Gemüt' (soul, mind, warmth). One of the most\nuntranslatable German words. Can describe places, people, or pace.

A: How was the walk?
B: So cozy and leisurely! No stress, just strolling.
A: Leisurely through the old town. Perfect.
B: Freiburg is a very comfortable city.

From 'Gemuet' (soul/warmth). 'Gemuetlich' describes a feeling of relaxed well-being. A cozy pub, a leisurely stroll, a warm atmosphere. As a TeKaMoLo modal adverb it describes HOW something is done: slowly, warmly, without rush.

When a sentence has multiple adverbs, German orders them like this:

Te = Temporal (when?)
Ka = Kausal (why?)
Mo = Modal (how?)
Lo = Lokal (where/to where?)

Example: 'Ich fahre morgen(Te) wegen des Regens(Ka) lieber(Mo) nach Freiburg(Lo).'

Memory aid: 'Tea, Coffee, Poppy, Lotto' (Tee, Kaffee, Mohn, Lotto).

This is the default order, not an absolute rule. Emphasis can shift elements, but TeKaMoLo sounds most natural.

TeKaMoLo is the default adverb order in German middle field (Mittelfeld) sentences. When multiple adverbs compete for position, this order sounds most natural to native speakers.

Temporal: morgen, gestern, heute, oft, manchmal
Kausal: wegen, deshalb, deswegen, aus Freude
Modal: gern, leider, irgendwie, vermutlich
Lokal: dort, hier, nach Freiburg, in der Altstadt

Example: 'Wir sind gestern(Te) aus Neugier(Ka) gemütlich(Mo) durch die Altstadt(Lo) flaniert.'

Note: Pronouns and known information tend to move left, new information moves right. TeKaMoLo applies when all else is equal.
Apply the four-position rule. Check each option to find the one with the correct order.
Which sentence follows the TeKaMoLo rule correctly?
A kausal element (WHY) between temporal (gestern) and modal (leider).
He did not go to Freiburg yesterday because of a headache, unfortunately.
When? Yesterday. Why? Out of curiosity. How? Comfortably. The verb goes at the end.
We strolled through the old town yesterday out of curiosity comfortably.
Identify which part of the compound gives it its gender. Then ask: which negation prefix creates the mildest opposite of an adjective?
Naturschutzgebiet: What is the base word, and which prefix could negate 'natürlich'?
From Gemuet (soul/warmth). Describes a relaxed, warm atmosphere. One of the most untranslatable German words.

Experience Lukas's concert with 'Fernweh'

Evening in Freiburg. A small venue in the old town. Fairy lights. The stage is tiny but the sound is big. Lukas's new band: drums, bass, keys, and Lukas on guitar and vocals. They are called 'Fernweh.'

The music starts. It is good. Really good. Lukas plays with his eyes closed, lost in the sound. The crowd leans in. Mia records on her phone. Hildi taps her foot. Verumius thinks: he found his way back.
He has become really good. The new band suits him.
Not bad. I would not have thought I would like something like this.
Lukas has played everywhere in Germany and has traveled a lot. That is why he knows every region.
state capital
Land (state) + Haupt (main) + Stadt (city).\nThree nouns in one capital city.

A: What is the state capital of Bavaria?
B: Munich.
A: And of Baden-Württemberg?
B: Stuttgart.

Land (state) + Haupt (main) + Stadt (city). Each of Germany's 16 Bundesländer has its Landeshauptstadt. Munich for Bavaria, Stuttgart for Baden-Württemberg, Dresden for Saxony. Three nouns in one capital.
coastal region
Küste (coast) + Region. Germany's Küstenregionen\nstretch along the North Sea and Baltic Sea.

A: Have you been to a coastal region?
B: Yes, on the Baltic Sea.
A: What is it like there?
B: Flat, windy, and beautiful.
Küste (coast) + Region. Germany's Küstenregionen stretch along the North Sea and Baltic Sea. Flat, windy, and home to Plattdeutsch speakers. Feminine because Region is feminine.
Northern Germany
Nord (north) + Deutschland. Hamburg, Bremen,\nSchleswig-Holstein. Flat, maritime, reserved.

A: What is Northern Germany like?
B: Flat and windy.
A: And the people?
B: A bit more reserved than in the south.
Nord (north) + Deutschland. Hamburg, Bremen, Schleswig-Holstein, Niedersachsen, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern. Flat landscapes, fishing villages, maritime culture. Neuter because Deutschland is neuter.
Southern Germany
Süd (south) + Deutschland. Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg.\nAlps, beer gardens, stronger dialects.

A: What is typical of Southern Germany?
B: Mountains, beer, and pretzels.
A: And the dialects?
B: Much stronger than in the north.
Süd (south) + Deutschland. Bavaria, Baden-Württemberg. Alps, beer gardens, Baroque churches, stronger dialects. Everything below the 'Weißwurst-Äquator.'
low mountain range
Mittel (middle) + Gebirge (mountain range). Germany's\nbackbone between 500 and 1,500 meters.

A: What is a Mittelgebirge?
B: A mountain range of medium height.
A: The Black Forest is one?
B: Yes, also the Harz and the Erzgebirge.
Mittel (middle) + Gebirge (mountain range). Germany's Mittelgebirge (Harz, Schwarzwald, Erzgebirge) are between 500 and 1,500m. Not the Alps, not the lowlands. The backbone of central Germany.
lowland plain
Tief (low/deep) + Ebene (plain). The Norddeutsche\nTiefebene stretches from the Netherlands to Poland.

A: Why is Northern Germany so flat?
B: Because of the lowland plain.
A: The North German Plain?
B: Yes, perfect for wind power and cycling.
Tief (low/deep) + Ebene (plain). The Norddeutsche Tiefebene stretches from the Netherlands to Poland. Flat as a table. Perfect for wind turbines and cycling.
river landscape
Fluss (river) + Landschaft (landscape). The Rhine,\nElbe, Danube, and Mosel shaped Germany's valleys.

A: Which river landscape is the most beautiful?
B: The Rhine Valley, with castles and vineyards.
A: The Mosel is also wonderful.
B: Every river landscape has its charm.
Fluss (river) + Landschaft (landscape). The Rhine, Elbe, Danube, and Mosel have shaped Germany's Flusslandschaften. Castles on cliffs, vineyards on slopes, trade routes in the valleys.
industrial area
Industrie + Gebiet (area). The Ruhrgebiet was Germany's\nindustrial heartland. Now a cultural region.

A: What is the Ruhrgebiet?
B: A large industrial area.
A: Are there only factories there?
B: No, today also museums and parks.
Industrie + Gebiet (area). The Ruhrgebiet (Ruhr area) was Germany's industrial heartland: coal, steel, factories. Now transformed into a cultural region with museums and parks.
Zugspitze (Germany's highest peak)
Zug (train/pull) + Spitze (peak). Germany's highest\nmountain at 2,962 meters, on the Austrian border.

A: How high is the Zugspitze?
B: 2,962 meters.
A: Can you ski there?
B: Yes, even in spring still.

Zug (train/pull/draft) + Spitze (peak/top). Germany's highest mountain at 2,962m. On the Austrian border. The name may come from avalanche channels ('Züge') that mark its face. Die Zugspitze is feminine.
onion dome tower
Zwiebel (onion) + Turm (tower). The iconic Bavarian\nchurch towers with bulbous domes.

A: Why do Bavarian churches have onion dome towers?
B: Nobody knows exactly.
A: Maybe because of the snow?
B: Possibly. The shape deflects snow.
Zwiebel (onion) + Turm (tower). The iconic church towers of Bavaria and Austria with their bulbous domes. Nobody knows exactly why the onion shape developed. Some say it sheds snow better.
guild, medieval craft association
From Old High German 'zumft' (what is fitting).\nBakers, blacksmiths, weavers each had their Zunft.

A: What was a guild?
B: A medieval craftsmen's association.
A: Do guilds still exist today?
B: As cultural associations, yes.
From Old High German 'zumft' (what is fitting). Bakers, blacksmiths, weavers: each had their Zunft with strict rules. Some Zünfte survive as cultural associations. Freiburg's Zunfthäuser still stand.
UNESCO World Heritage Site
UNESCO + Welt (world) + Erbe (heritage). Germany\nhas 52 Welterbe sites. Neuter because Erbe is neuter.

A: Is Cologne Cathedral a UNESCO World Heritage Site?
B: Yes, since 1996.
A: How many World Heritage Sites does Germany have?
B: 52, from castles to the Wadden Sea.
UNESCO + Welt (world) + Erbe (heritage). Germany has 52 Welterbe sites: Cologne Cathedral, Bamberg's Altstadt, the Bauhaus buildings, the Wadden Sea. Neuter because Erbe (heritage) is neuter.
to house, to accommodate
Be- + herbergen (from Herberge, inn). Not separable.\nThe be- prefix makes it transitive.

A: What does this museum house?
B: Over 10,000 artworks.
A: The city accommodates many tourists.
B: Especially in summer.

Be- + herbergen (from Herberge, inn/shelter). To house, to accommodate. 'Das Museum beherbergt 10.000 Werke.' Not separable. The be- prefix makes it transitive. You beherbergen guests, collections, or secrets.
to erect, to build
Er- + richten (to direct/set up). More formal than\n'bauen.' Used for significant structures.

A: When was this castle erected?
B: In the 12th century.
A: Who erected it?
B: Presumably a local prince.
Er- + richten (to direct/set up). To erect, to build. 'Die Burg wurde im 12. Jahrhundert errichtet.' More formal than 'bauen.' Used for significant structures. Not separable.
to restore
From Latin 'restaurare.' Same word in English\nand French. Used for historic buildings and art.

A: Is the palace being restored?
B: Yes, for three years.
A: Restoring costs a lot of money.
B: But it is worth it for the cultural heritage.
From Latin 'restaurare' (to restore). 'Das Schloss wurde aufwendig restauriert.' Same word in English and French. Germans restaurieren historic buildings meticulously.
to present, to showcase
From Latin 'praesentare.' Same word across European\nlanguages. Formal but widely used.

A: The city showcases its cultural heritage.
B: How does it showcase it?
A: With city tours and museums.
B: That is a good strategy.
From Latin 'praesentare' (to present). 'Die Stadt präsentiert ihr Kulturerbe.' Same word across European languages. Formal but widely used.
everywhere
Über (over) + all (all). A lokal adverb.\nLiterally 'over all' places.

A: Where are there half-timbered houses?
B: Everywhere in Germany.
A: Everywhere?
B: Well, especially in the center and south.
Über (over) + all (all). Everywhere. A lokal adverb. 'Überall in Deutschland gibt es schöne Orte.' Literally 'over all' places. The broadest possible location.
somewhere
Irgend (some-) + wo (where). Part of the irgend-\nfamily. The sister of 'nirgendwo' (nowhere).

A: Where is the café?
B: Somewhere in the old town.
A: Somewhere? That does not help me.
B: Near the cathedral, I think.
Irgend (some-) + wo (where). Somewhere. 'Irgendwo in Süddeutschland.' Part of the 'irgend-' family. Vague on purpose. The sister of 'nirgendwo' (nowhere).

Which sentence has the correct TeKaMoLo order? 'Lukas has...'
This grammatical term names the element that always comes last in a compound and controls its gender.
In a German compound noun, the ___ determines the grammatical gender.
It is un-believable that heritage was des-troyed. But much was also re-stored.
It is unbelievable that so much cultural heritage was destroyed. But much was also restored.
The sentence orders adverbs correctly, uses a compound adjective, and a probability adverb.
Which B2 constructs appear in: 'The medieval castle was presumably erected in the 13th century because of its strategic location high above the valley'?
Test everything from Unit 34. Prove you can decompose compounds, negate with prefixes, and order adverbs.
Decompose compounds into their parts
Negate with un-, miss-, and zer-
Order adverbs according to TeKaMoLo
to colonize, to settle (an area)
Be- + siedeln (to settle). Transitive: you besiedeln\na place. Not separable. Historical vocabulary.

A: Who settled the Rhineland?
B: The Romans, 2,000 years ago.
A: And later?
B: Germanic tribes settled it afterward.
Be- + siedeln (to settle). 'Die Römer besiedelten das Rheinland.' The be- prefix makes it transitive: you besiedeln a place. Not separable. Historical vocabulary.
to roam through, to wander across
Durch (through) + streifen (to roam). Not separable.\nMore poetic than 'durchqueren.' No fixed route.

A: We roamed through the Black Forest.
B: How long were you on the go?
A: Three days, without a fixed destination.
B: That sounds wonderful.
Durch (through) + streifen (to roam). To roam through, to wander across. 'Den Schwarzwald durchstreifen.' Not separable (inseparable durch-). More poetic than 'durchqueren.' Implies no fixed route.
to maintain, to keep in repair
Instand (in good condition) + halten (to keep).\nSeparable. The verb for heritage preservation.

A: Who maintains the castle?
B: The municipality.
A: That costs a lot of money.
B: Yes, but it is important for the cultural heritage.
Instand (in good condition) + halten (to keep). To maintain, to keep in repair. 'Das Bauwerk muss instandgehalten werden.' Separable. Every Denkmalschutz (monument protection) office instandhält.
to redesign, to transform
Um (re-/around) + gestalten (to design). Separable.\nThe verb for urban renewal and redesign.

A: The old town was redesigned.
B: How was it redesigned?
A: New pedestrian zones and green spaces.
B: That sounds like a good transformation.
Um (re-/around) + gestalten (to design/shape). To redesign, to transform. 'Die Altstadt wurde umgestaltet.' Separable. You umgestalten what already exists into something new.
to drive around (insep.) / to knock down (sep.)
Two meanings depending on stress. Inseparable 'umFAHREN'\n= drive around. Separable 'UMfahren' = knock down.

A: I drove around the construction site.
B: Good, then you went around it.
A: Last week I almost knocked over a bollard.
B: That would not have been so good!
Two meanings depending on stress. 'UMfahren' (separable, to knock down by driving over) vs. 'umFAHREN' (inseparable, to drive around). A famous German grammar trap.
nowhere
Nirgend (nowhere) + wo (where). The negative twin\nof 'irgendwo' and 'überall.' A complete set.

A: Nowhere is it more beautiful than here.
B: You say that everywhere.
A: But here I mean it.
B: Nowhere else are there such mountains.
Nirgend (nowhere) + wo (where). Nowhere. 'Nirgendwo ist es schöner als hier.' The negative twin of 'irgendwo' (somewhere) and 'überall' (everywhere). Three location adverbs forming a complete set.
downstream
Fluss (river) + abwärts (downward). A lokal adverb.\nOpposite: flussaufwärts (upstream).

A: Where are we heading?
B: Downstream, toward Heidelberg.
A: And upstream?
B: There lies Basel, in Switzerland.
Fluss (river) + abwärts (downward). Downstream. 'Flussabwärts liegt Heidelberg.' The opposite: 'flussaufwärts' (upstream). Directional compound specific to river geography.
unbelievable, incredible
Un- + glaublich (believable). The most common un-\nword in everyday German. Also an intensifier.

A: The view is incredible!
B: Incredibly beautiful, yes.
A: Unbelievable that we are here.
B: Sometimes reality is unbelievable.
Un- + glaublich (believable). Unbelievable, incredible. 'Unglaublich schön!' The most common un- word in everyday German. Used both literally and as an intensifier.
impossible
Un- + möglich (possible). Also describes rude behavior:\n'Er ist unmöglich' (He is insufferable).

A: That is impossible!
B: Why impossible?
A: The task is simply too hard.
B: Hard, but not impossible.
Un- + möglich (possible). Impossible. 'Das ist unmöglich.' Also used to describe rude behavior: 'Er ist unmöglich' (He is impossible/insufferable). Double duty.
absolutely, by all means
Un- + bedingt (conditional). Paradoxically, un- here\nintensifies rather than negates. One of the trickiest.

A: You absolutely must come!
B: Really? Absolutely?
A: Yes, it will definitely be great.
B: Then I will definitely come.
Un- + bedingt (conditional). Unconditional, absolutely, by all means. 'Du musst unbedingt kommen!' Paradoxically, un- here intensifies rather than negates. One of the trickiest un- words.
incomprehensible
Un- + verständlich (understandable). When Hochdeutsch\nspeakers meet strong dialect, this is the word.

A: His dialect is incomprehensible.
B: For me too.
A: That is completely incomprehensible.
B: You need years to understand that.
Un- + verständlich (understandable). Incomprehensible. 'Sein Dialekt ist unverständlich.' When Hochdeutsch speakers meet strong dialect, this is the word they reach for.
unbearable, intolerable
Un- + erträglich (bearable). Three layers of word\nformation: ertragen + -lich + un-.

A: The heat is unbearable.
B: Yes, almost 40 degrees.
A: Unbearably hot.
B: Let us go to the outdoor pool.
Un- + erträglich (bearable). Unbearable, intolerable. 'Die Hitze ist unerträglich.' Ertragen (to endure) + -lich + un-. Three layers of word formation in one adjective.
to shatter, to break into pieces
Zer- + brechen (to break). Stronger than 'brechen.'\nZer- implies complete destruction.

A: The glass shattered.
B: Into a thousand pieces.
A: Shattering is final.
B: Yes, you cannot repair that.
Zer- + brechen (to break). To shatter, to break into pieces. 'Das Glas ist zerbrochen.' Stronger than 'brechen' (break). Zer- implies complete destruction, not just damage.
to tear apart, to rip to pieces
Zer- + reißen (to tear). Violent destruction by tearing.\nThe zer- prefix escalates the action.

A: He tore the letter apart.
B: Why?
A: He was angry.
B: Torn paper everywhere.
Zer- + reißen (to tear). To tear apart, to rip to pieces. 'Er hat den Brief zerrissen.' Violent destruction by tearing. The zer- prefix escalates the action.
to decay, to disintegrate
Zer- + fallen (to fall). Slow, passive destruction.\nTime zerfällt things. Inevitable decay.

A: The old castle is decaying.
B: Nobody maintains it.
A: Decay is a slow process.
B: But just as destructive as shattering.
Zer- + fallen (to fall). To decay, to disintegrate, to fall apart. 'Die alte Burg zerfällt.' A slow, passive destruction. Time zerfällt things. Not sudden like zerbrechen, but inevitable.
to disassemble, to take apart
Zer- + legen (to lay). Can be constructive: a mechanic\nzerlegt an engine to fix it. Also for words.

A: Can you take the word apart?
B: Sure: Natur + Schutz + Gebiet.
A: Taking apart helps with understanding.
B: Every long word can be taken apart.
Zer- + legen (to lay). To disassemble, to take apart, to dissect. 'Das Wort in Teile zerlegen' (to dissect the word into parts). Can be constructive: a mechanic zerlegt an engine to fix it.
gender, sex
Neuter noun. Plural: die Geschlechter.\nIn grammar: grammatisches Geschlecht = der/die/das.

A: What is the gender of 'Tuer'?
B: Feminine. Die Tuer.
A: And 'Haus'?
B: Neuter. Das Haus. The base word determines the gender.

In compound nouns, the Grundwort (last part) determines the grammatical gender of the whole word. 'Haustuer' gets its gender from 'Tuer' (die), not from 'Haus' (das). Knowing this rule helps decode any German compound.
joyful, cheerful
Freude (joy) + -ig. The -ig suffix creates adjectives.\n'Freudig' describes manner or mood.

A: How was the hike?
B: Wonderful! We walked joyfully through the forest.
A: Hiking joyfully is the best.
B: Yes, with good weather everything is fun.

Freude (joy) + -ig. Same root as 'froh' (glad) and 'froehlich' (cheerful), but 'freudig' emphasizes the outward expression of joy. 'Ein freudiger Anlass' (a joyful occasion). Works as both adjective and modal adverb in TeKaMoLo position.
Un- = cannot be. Miss- = went wrong. Zer- = completely fallen apart. Three levels of darkness.
'Impossible, failed, decayed.' Which description fits?
The base word determines the property that makes it der, die, or das.
The base word determines the ___ of the compound noun.
When? This morning. Why? Because of the weather. How? Joyfully. The verb goes at the end.
We hiked through the Black Forest this morning because of the nice weather joyfully.
When um- is inseparable (umFAHREN), it means 'to drive around.' Separable (UMfahren) means 'to knock over.'
'I drove around the construction site.' What happened?
worthy, dignified
Forms many compounds: sehenswürdig (worth seeing), denkwürdig (memorable), würdevoll (dignified).
He is a worthy successor.
Related to die Würde (dignity), the very first word in the German constitution: Die Würde des Menschen ist unantastbar.
The middle element of this compound means deserving or meriting something. Related to 'Würde' (dignity) but functioning as an adjective suffix.
The tourist guide explains: 'This building is a real attraction.' The middle element means ___.
It is un-believable that one can possibly (modal) discover beautiful places everywhere (local).
It is unbelievable that one can possibly discover beautiful places everywhere in Germany.
'Die Bildung, die Freiheit, die Möglichkeit, die Freundschaft.' The pattern never breaks.
Words ending in -ung, -heit, -keit, and -schaft are always...
Reflect on Germany's diversity. Master all suffix families. Review everything from Unit 34. Appreciate the journey.

Reflect on the journey through Germany

Sunday morning. The Black Forest. A viewpoint above the mist. The trees are dark and ancient. The valleys below are hidden in white. The sun is just touching the treetops. The group stands together, quiet for once.
This is also Germany. Not just Berlin, not just grammar. Federal states, dialects, mountains, rivers, castles. An entire country.
You have learned a lot on this trip, Verumius. Not just words.
And you won the chain word game. Stage fright. I will not forget that.
Next time we will drive to the North Sea. Then you will learn Low German.
Possibly I will not understand anything anymore. But somehow I will manage.
to disregard, to ignore disrespectfully
Miss- + achten (to regard/respect). Stronger than\n'nicht beachten.' Implies willful wrongdoing.

A: He disregards the rules.
B: That is dangerous.
A: Disregarding rules has consequences.
B: Yes, especially in traffic.
Miss- + achten (to regard/respect). To disregard, to ignore disrespectfully. 'Die Regeln missachten' (to disregard the rules). Stronger than 'nicht beachten.' Miss- implies willful wrongdoing.
to fail, to not succeed
Miss- + lingen (to succeed). The opposite of 'gelingen.'\nNot separable. Past participle: misslungen.

A: The attempt failed.
B: What went wrong?
A: Everything. It failed completely.
B: Then we will try again.

Miss- + lingen (to succeed). To fail, to not succeed. 'Der Versuch ist misslungen.' The opposite of 'gelingen' (to succeed). Not separable. The past participle is unusual: misslungen (not gemisslungen).
misunderstanding
Miss- + Verständnis (understanding). Neuter because\nVerständnis is neuter. The noun of missverstehen.

A: There was a misunderstanding.
B: What kind of misunderstanding?
A: I thought we were meeting at three.
B: No, at four. Typical misunderstanding.
Miss- + Verständnis (understanding). A misunderstanding. 'Es gab ein Missverständnis.' Das Missverständnis is neuter because Verständnis is neuter. The noun form of missverstehen.
to misuse, to abuse
Miss- + brauchen (to use/need). Not separable.\nThe miss- prefix corrupts 'use' into 'misuse.'

A: Power must not be abused.
B: That is true.
A: Abusing trust is terrible.
B: Yes, that destroys friendships.
Miss- + brauchen (to use/need). To misuse, to abuse. 'Macht missbrauchen' (to abuse power). Not separable. The miss- prefix corrupts the neutral 'use' into harmful 'misuse.'
freedom
Frei (free) + -heit. The -heit suffix creates abstract\nnouns from adjectives. Always feminine.

A: Freedom is priceless.
B: Yes, the freedom to travel.
A: The freedom to learn.
B: The freedom to make mistakes.
Frei (free) + -heit. Freedom. 'Die Freiheit ist unbezahlbar.' The -heit suffix creates abstract nouns from adjectives. Always feminine. Germany's most treasured post-1989 word.
beauty
Schön (beautiful) + -heit. Same suffix as Freiheit.\nThe abstract quality of being beautiful.

A: The beauty of the landscape is breathtaking.
B: Yes, this beauty exists nowhere else.
A: Beauty is in the eye of the beholder.
B: But here everyone agrees.
Schön (beautiful) + -heit. Beauty. 'Die Schönheit der Landschaft.' Same suffix as Freiheit. The abstract quality of being beautiful. Always feminine.
possibility
Möglich (possible) + -keit. The -keit suffix appears\nafter -lich and -ig adjectives. Always feminine.

A: There are many possibilities.
B: Which possibility do you choose?
A: The best possibility.
B: Sometimes there is no good possibility.
Möglich (possible) + -keit. Possibility. 'Es gibt viele Möglichkeiten.' The -keit suffix appears after -lich and -ig adjectives. The world of what COULD happen. Always feminine.
ability, skill
Fähig (capable) + -keit. Same pattern as Möglichkeit.\nThe -ig adjective takes -keit, not -heit.

A: Language skills are important.
B: What skills do you have?
A: I speak three languages.
B: That is a great ability.
Fähig (capable) + -keit. Ability, skill. 'Sprachliche Fähigkeiten' (language skills). Same pattern as Möglichkeit. The -ig adjective takes -keit, not -heit. Always feminine.
landscape
Land + -schaft. The -schaft suffix creates collective\nor abstract nouns. Always feminine.

A: The German landscape is diverse.
B: From coast to Alps.
A: Every landscape has its charm.
B: Especially the Black Forest.
Land + -schaft. Landscape. 'Die deutsche Landschaft ist vielfältig.' The -schaft suffix creates collective or abstract nouns. Same suffix in Freundschaft, Wirtschaft, Gesellschaft. Always feminine.
friendship
Freund (friend) + -schaft. The -schaft suffix implies\na collective state. This road trip strengthened theirs.

A: Our friendship is strong.
B: Yes, even stronger through this trip.
A: Friendship needs shared experiences.
B: Like a road trip through Germany.
Freund (friend) + -schaft. Friendship. 'Unsere Freundschaft ist stark.' The -schaft suffix implies a collective state or condition. This road trip strengthened their Freundschaft.
wealth, richness
Reich (rich) + -tum. The -tum suffix usually creates\nmasculine nouns. Exception: das Wachstum.

A: The richness of German culture.
B: Not just material wealth.
A: Cultural richness is more important.
B: Traditions, languages, landscapes.
Reich (rich) + -tum. Wealth, richness. 'Der Reichtum der deutschen Kultur.' The -tum suffix usually creates masculine nouns (Reichtum, Irrtum). Exception: das Eigentum, das Wachstum.
growth
Wachsen (to grow) + -tum. Neuter, an exception to\nthe mostly-masculine -tum pattern.

A: Economic growth is important.
B: But not at the expense of the environment.
A: Sustainable growth is the goal.
B: Growth with responsibility.
Wachsen (to grow) + -tum. Growth. 'Wirtschaftswachstum' (economic growth). Das Wachstum is neuter, an exception to the mostly-masculine -tum pattern.
result, outcome
Er- + geben (to give) + -nis. The -nis suffix usually\ncreates neuter nouns. What comes out of giving effort.

A: What is the result of the trip?
B: New words, new friends.
A: A good result.
B: The best result of all.
Er- + geben (to give) + -nis. A result, an outcome. 'Das Ergebnis der Reise.' The -nis suffix creates neuter nouns (usually). What comes out of (er-) giving effort.
knowledge, awareness
Kennen (to know) + -nis. Feminine, unlike most -nis\nnouns. One of the exceptions.

A: Do you have knowledge of German?
B: Yes, good knowledge.
A: Language skills are valuable.
B: They open doors.
Kennen (to know) + -nis. Knowledge, awareness. 'Sprachkenntnisse' (language knowledge) on a CV. Die Kenntnis is feminine, unlike most -nis nouns. One of the exceptions.
feasible, doable
Machen (to make/do) + -bar. The -bar suffix means\n'able to be X-ed.' Same as English '-able.'

A: Is that feasible?
B: Yes, absolutely feasible.
A: In two days?
B: Difficult, but doable.
Machen (to make/do) + -bar. Feasible, doable. 'Das ist machbar!' The -bar suffix creates adjectives meaning 'able to be X-ed.' Same as English '-able': machbar = makeable = doable.
solvable
Lösen (to solve) + -bar. Every puzzle, every grammar\nquestion: lösbar. The optimistic -bar suffix.

A: Is the problem solvable?
B: Every problem is solvable.
A: Even grammar problems?
B: Especially those. With practice.
Lösen (to solve) + -bar. Solvable. 'Das Problem ist lösbar.' Every puzzle, every Stau, every grammar question: lösbar. The optimistic -bar suffix.
Most -tum nouns follow the masculine pattern, but one exception in the list comes from a verb of increase rather than a noun of state. Which option represents process or increase?
Most -tum nouns are masculine (der Reichtum). Which is an exception?
The plan did not succeed. The miss- prefix turns success (gelingen) into failure.
The plan unfortunately failed.
priceless, invaluable
Un- + bezahlbar (payable). What no amount of money\ncan equal. The -bar suffix: bezahlen + -bar = payable.

A: Was the trip expensive?
B: Financially yes, but the experience is priceless.
A: Friendship is priceless.
B: The most valuable thing there is.

Un- + bezahlbar (payable). What cannot be paid for. The -bar suffix creates adjectives meaning 'able to be X-ed': bezahlbar (payable), machbar (doable), loesbar (solvable). Adding un- negates: unpayable, therefore priceless.
The beauty of the landscape and the friendship of the people make the trip priceless (un- + bezahlbar).
The beauty of the landscape and the friendship of the people make this trip priceless.
This category describes the manner of an action. Position 3 in the four-slot rule. Think: not when, not why, not where.
Verumius says: 'Possibly I will not understand anything anymore.' What does 'möglicherweise' represent in TeKaMoLo?
